name: Cerium
name_origin: Named after the dwarf planet Ceres, which had been discovered two years before the element.

discovery.discoverer: Jons Jakob Berzelius and Wilhelm Hisinger; independently by Martin Heinrich Klaproth

occurrence.abundance_summary: Most abundant rare-earth element in the Earth's crust (~66 ppm), more common than copper; principal ores are bastnaesite and monazite.

compounds.name: Cerium(IV) oxide (ceria)
compounds.summary: Pale-yellow solid used as a polishing agent for glass, an automotive catalytic converter component, and in solid oxide fuel cells.

compounds.name: Cerium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: Catalyst component and phosphor host material; readily oxidises to CeO2 in air.

compounds.name: Cerium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Lewis acid catalyst used in organic synthesis (Luche reduction) and as a precursor to other cerium compounds.

compounds.name: Ceric ammonium nitrate (CAN)
compounds.summary: Strong one-electron oxidant widely used in organic chemistry and as an etchant for chromium and copper films.

uses: Glass and gemstone polishing powder (cerium oxide)
Yellow colourant and decolouriser for glass (UV blocker)
Misch metal in lighter flints (~50% cerium)
Carbon-arc lamps for film projection and searchlights
reactivity_summary: Highly reactive iron-grey metal; pyrophoric when finely divided and tarnishes rapidly in air. Reacts with water (slowly cold, quickly hot) and dissolves readily in dilute acids. Notable for accessible Ce(III)/Ce(IV) redox chemistry.
isotopes_summary: Cerium has four stable or long-lived naturally occurring isotopes: Ce-136, Ce-138, Ce-140 (most abundant ~88.45%), and Ce-142. Ce-144 (half-life 285 days) is an important fission product used in radioisotope thermoelectric generators historically.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Cerium
IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements (2021)
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd Edition
